step_1_context.text: Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset. Not only did she win the race, but she also broke the record.

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: Inversion means putting the auxiliary verb before the subject, usually after negative or restrictive adverbs at the start of a sentence.
step_2_explanation.formula: Negative adverb + auxiliary + subject + main verb. Common triggers: Never, Rarely, Seldom, Not only, Hardly, No sooner.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: Inversion is only used when the negative word starts the sentence. Compare: 'I have never seen this' (normal) vs 'Never have I seen this' (inverted).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: Rarely ___ such talent in a young musician.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: After 'Rarely' at the start, use inverted order: auxiliary (have) + subject (I) + past participle.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: Not only ___ the exam, but she also got the highest score.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: After 'Not only', use: did + subject + base verb.
